findings.message: plugin.json has no 'hooks' field pointing to hooks/hooks.json; CLAUDE.md states the manifest 'points to hooks' but no such reference exists, risking silent hook registration failure
plugin.json has no field linking to hooks/hooks.json; CLAUDE.md states manifest 'points to hooks' but no such link exists — hooks may not register on install
Template instruction 'allowed-tools: [Relevant tools based on workflow]' in Step 6 tells Claude to pick tools vaguely when generating child skill files
'appropriate heading' in Step 7a.1 is vague — does not specify which heading the learning should go under
'relevant section header' in Step 7a is vague — does not specify how to determine which section is relevant
'where they make semantic sense' in Step 7b provides no criteria for placement decisions

Context injection '!python3 "$(dirname "$(dirname "$(readlink -f "$0")")")/.../read_queue.py"' uses $0 to find plugin root; in Claude Code's ! context $0 is the shell interpreter, not the skill file, making this silently fall back to '|| echo "[]"'
Inline Python snippets alternate between 'from lib.semantic_detector import' and 'from scripts.lib.reflect_utils import'; the short form would fail unless scripts/ is on sys.path
reflect.md uses $0-based path resolution for plugin root while skip-reflect.md and reflect-skills.md use CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT env var — two competing patterns in the same plugin
'Read' is declared in allowed-tools but no step in the task instructions calls the Read tool; all implementation uses Bash (python3 scripts/read_queue.py)
Arbitrary user input stored in $PROMPT via 'echo "$INPUT" | jq -r ...' and then piped to grep; no eval or exec so risk is low, but embedded newlines can cause unexpected shell behavior
Bash tool command captured via 'echo "$INPUT" | jq -r ...' into $COMMAND, used only in string comparison; no exec risk but unquoted $INPUT is fragile
User message text from session JSONL files is interpolated into a Claude CLI prompt via subprocess.run; malicious session content could attempt prompt injection against the API call
